title: Quantum Ghost Spectroscopy Reveals Hidden Electronic Coherence in Molecular Aggregates
authors: Mingran Zhang, Yihe Xu, Vladislav V. Yakovlev, Zhedong Zhang
abstract: Ultrafast spectroscopy of molecular systems is fundamentally constrained by the Fourier uncertainty principle: high temporal resolution smears out electronic state signatures, while high spectral resolution obscures dynamic information. Here we overcome this limitation using time-resolved quantum ghost spectroscopy (tr-QGS) with entangled photon pairs, which enables independent control of temporal and spectral scales. We apply this approach to perylene bismide (PBI-1) trimers for energy transfer,by combining a quantum description of light-molecule interaction with time-dependent density matrix renormalization group (TD-DMRG) simulations. This explicitly includes five vibrational modes and nonadiabatic coupling between electronic states. Our simulations reveal that tr-QGS uniquely captures electronic coherence oscillating at 0.7 eV for >50 fs, a signature of nonadiabatic coupling that was obscured in conventional time-resolved fluorescence due to Fourier-limited broadening. Moreover, we observe a direct transfer from electronic to vibrational coherence at 200 fs, providing real-time visualization of vibronic relaxation pathways. The entangled photon correlation enables a sensitivity below the shot-noise limit and suppresses photobleaching artifacts that plague classical measurements. These results establish tr-QGS as a transformative tool for interrogating nonadiabatic dynamics in molecular aggregates, light-harvesting complexes, and photocatalysts, offering a route to reveal quantum coherence in chemistry with unprecedented time-energy precision.
summary: This paper introduces a quantum ghost spectroscopy technique using entangled photons to overcome the Fourier uncertainty limit in ultrafast spectroscopy. By decoupling temporal and spectral resolutions, the authors reveal hidden electronic-to-vibrational coherence transfer in molecular aggregates. This approach provides a new way to observe high-frequency quantum dynamics that are otherwise obscured in classical measurements.
main_problem: Conventional ultrafast spectroscopy is limited by the Fourier uncertainty principle, creating a trade-off between temporal and spectral resolution that obscures hidden electronic and vibrational coherence dynamics.
main_result: Time-resolved quantum ghost spectroscopy (tr-QGS) using entangled photons enables independent control of time and energy scales, successfully revealing previously hidden 0.7 eV electronic coherence and the subsequent transfer to vibrational coherence.
method: The study combines a theoretical framework for tr-QGS using fourth-order perturbative expansion with Time-Dependent Density Matrix Renormalization Group (TD-DMRG) simulations.
model_or_system: The system consists of perylene bismide (PBI-1) trimers in a dilute solvent, modeled with a Hamiltonian including electronic states (S1, S2), five vibrational modes, and nonadiabatic vibronic coupling.
key_observables: Electronic coherence (rho_S1,S2), population dynamics (S2 to S1 internal conversion), and vibrational coherence (mode position dynamics).
important_parameters: Electronic energy difference of ~0.7 eV, temporal scales of 5-50 fs for electronic and 200-300 fs for vibrational coherence, and sub-shot-noise sensitivity.
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the validity of fourth-order perturbative expansion and assumes the initial state is created by the signal photon pulse.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the molecular structure, potential energy surfaces, and the tr-QGS experimental setup; Figure 2 shows coherence dynamics; Figure 6 illustrates the comparison between time- and frequency-gated signals.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Fourier limitation problem, proposes the tr-QGS platform, details the TD-DMRG simulation methodology, presents results on population and coherence dynamics, and discusses broader applications.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a quantum-enhanced spectroscopic technique that bypasses fundamental classical limits (Fourier uncertainty) to resolve ultrafast nonadiabatic dynamics, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Entangled-light spectroscopy. (a) Molecular structure of PBI-1 trimer. (b) Schematic diagram of involved PESs of PBI-1 molecule. (e1, e2) denotes a pair of nearest- neighbor monomers, one is in state e1 and the other is in e2. Coordinates 1 and 2 are the same vibrational mode inside monomer 1 and 2 respectively. (c) Proposed experimental setup. A pump pulse enters a nonlinear crystal to generate two light beams s (signal) and i (idler) which are entangled with an entanglement time τ. Signal photons, s, interact with molecules whereas photons in i arm propagate freely with a time delay T. With respect to s-arm photons, molecules emit fluorescence which is collected by an...

Figure 2: Quantum dynamics of PBI-1 trimers. (a) Populations at S1 and S2 states. (b) Electronic coherence ρS1,S2. (c) Mean dimensionless position of the jth vibrational mode. Total evolution time is set to 500 fs with a step 0.166 fs.
Figure 3: Quantum correlation function |Φ(ωs, ωi)| between s and i photons. (a) Entangle- ment time τ = 7 fs (b) τ = 200 fs.

Figure 4: Tr-QGS signal as a function of temporal gate parameter τ, where ω is emission frequency and T is the delay between s and i photons. (a) τ = 7 fs. (b) τ = 50 fs. (c) τ = 200 fs. Parameters are γ = 0.41 eV, ω+ =4.7 eV, in line with recent experiments.46 Molecular parameters are reproduced from Ref.44 The change of τ correlates to the three green circles in the horizontal line in Figure 1(e). All signals at ω > 2.6 eV have been rescaled by a factor of 50.

Figure 5: Tr-QGS signal as a function of the spectral gate parameters. (a) γd = 0 eV. (b) γd = 0.41 eV. (c) γd = 1.03 eV. Parameters are τ = 7 fs, γ0 = 0.41 eV. Others are the same as Figure 4. The change of γd correlates to the three green circles in the vertical line in Figure 1(e).
